sentences.text: I'm going upstairs.
It's in there.
So I go upstairs.
[INAUDIBLE]
Yes.
Yes, the work that-- I did all kind of outside works.
Because I wasn't really a-- an expert in this kind of work.
So first of all, there was the machines.
We have to bring in machines.
The machines were very heavy.
We have to be- we have to hold a piece of-- hole of wooden holes So a bunch of people were pushing that, pushing that base until we got in our machine to the things.
We got in the machine.
Inside the hole, there has to be put up.
So that was-- take a lot of days to works to put up the machines, too.
And then also I worked on the outside when we put some potatoes for the winter time so they shouldn't freeze.
So you put them in places and cover them with dirty.
That was also a lot of-- a big job to do.
And then also for [NON-ENGLISH], which people came-- this is also important.
There was a camp near-- near Auschwitz.
It's called Golleschau, a small camp.
And they had some people by-- when they liquidated, they put them in two cars, train cars.
And they were going from camp to camp.
And no camp wanted to take him in.
There were 90-- 90 people, I think-- something like 90 people.
And finally, when they came to Brunnlitz, Schindler, says he's going to take them in.
So I was taken out to open up the cars.
The cars were-- that was in January 1945.
The cars were on the-- on the road for three weeks,
They were frozen.
You couldn't open up a door.
Finally, they put some fire under the trains.
And with hedges, with all kind of instruments, we opened up the doors.
We opened up the doors.
That was a scene-- terrible.
Something terrible-- there were 90 people laying one on top of the other one, frozen, some of them dead.
None of them could move.
There was some of them that weighed 40, 45 pounds, 50 pounds the most.
So finally, we have to take them apart.
Some of them-- the ones that were alive they put on stretchers, and they took them in.
Mrs.
Schindler took care.
She helped took care on them.
She went to town, and she bought on the black market some medicine, some special food for them.
And some of them survived, some of them died.
And a lot of them-- so Schindler bought a little place there.
He made a little cemetery for them.
And they buried them over there.
Also, when we arrived in Płaszow-- I forgot this.
When we arrived in Płaszow, the first Yom Kipper, which is the holiest day, they lined up 95 people in an open-- open ground.
And they were laying completely naked, alive.
And we went to Madrich.
And they chased us fast, fast to go.
We ran by.
And then they opened up machine guns, and they shot all these people in this grave alive when they were laying alive there.
That was the first day when we arrived, two weeks after we arrived, two or three weeks after we arrived in Płaszow.
There was another scene.
They were shooting every day.
Every day was shooting.
Like you said in the movie, this is exactly what happened.
In Brunnlitz was nothing to-- things, just day by day.
We didn't know when the day ends, when the day starts.
Because they were all day long.
First of all, the windows were sealed with black paper.
Because they were afraid for the English American planes to bomb.
So there was no-- shouldn't see any lights.
Hot was in this [INAUDIBLE] was terrible hot.
And that's the way we slept, one on top of the other one.
If you lay down, if you fall asleep, you didn't know where you'd get up in the morning.
Because sometimes, the people were pushing each other.
So we were sitting like this.
And then we fell asleep.
And we fall asleep.
So moving around, was-- it was impossible.
It's impossible to describe.
No, not food.
Was very, very little food.
Soup was just like [NON-ENGLISH].
Yet, the little-- a little-- I don't remember even, a little, small piece of bread during the day.
I don't remember even the food.
Because it was so-- so hectic that we didn't pay attention to it.
We didn't eat any food anymore.
Because it was all day long was chasing and chasing and chasing.
Where?
In Brunnlitz, yes.
We made-- we sent out-- according to what I heard, they send out the first shipment.
It was out the last week of the war.
And some people say none of them was usable.
I didn't know these things, because I-- didn't know-- I didn't' have any [INAUDIBLE].
Then the last week before the end of the war-- I think it was on Tuesday-- Schindler came.
And first of all, it was one day when Roosevelt died.
[NON-ENGLISH].
This means the Jew father died.
That's what she said.
So we heard that Roosevelt died.
Otherwise, we didn't know anything about.
Then the last week, Schindler came out.
And he says-- first of all, on Tuesday evening, I saw there was a-- a engineer, a German engineer.
He went-- when he walked into the camp, he never looked anybody from the Jewish people into the eyes.
He went in straight.
And he gives some orders to this Jewish engineer.
And the Jewish engineer gave the orders to the people to work.
And he never talked to anybody.
There was a doctor by the name Leftkovitch.
He was from Lodz.
And on the same Tuesday evening, he came in.
And he says to this Doctor Leftkovich, he wanted to shake hands with him and say goodbye to him, because he was leaving.
So this Doctor Leftkovich says yesterday, you wouldn't shake hands with me.
Tonight, I don't want to shake hands with you.
Because we knew already that the war is getting to end.
So he didn't say anything and he walked out.
In the same evening, I saw the SS packing the trucks, and they're leaving.
So Schindler came out and says, the war is almost over.
We don't have to work.
But he warned us not to go out and not to take revenge.
Everything will be taken care.
We don't have to worry about it.
So it was the next morning, I went with Margot.
There was a big back yard.
I says to Margot, let's go out and lay a bit in the sun, because we didn't have to work anymore.
And on top was the highway.
So we saw the Germans running back and forth, back and forth with things and shooting.
They were shooting all over-- things from the Russians from the one side and the Germans were running away.
And the next day, it was the 8th of May, Thursday the 8th of May.
We heard already the officially that the German government surrendered.
On Friday morning, a Russian came.
And he says to us-- he came in and says, a camp?
A camp?
Yes.
He says, you are free.
You don't have to worry anymore.
You are-- so they-- some people start asking him.
What's in Poland?
So he says, I just came from Poland, he says.
And we shouldn't go to Poland, he says.
But in the same day, Friday, Margot wanted to go out from the camp.
So she went out a little bit with some other couple of women.
And then next to the camp was a building.
There was already the white flag hanging out, because they were occupied by German people.
And this German woman came out.
And she saw Margot.
She says, would you like to move in to me?
I have a room for you.
So she says, I have to ask my husband.
And she came back in.
And she asked me.
And I says, yes, why should I sleep in the camp if I can go into a room?
The woman took us in, because she was afraid they're going to send in some people anyway to her.
So we came in.
And then I told her that if she had another room, so my brother Moishe and other man came in.
They moved into the other room.
And she opened up the kitchen.
She says, you want to cook here?
You want to do something?
So Margot-- and we had-- from this factory where Schindler took over, there was a Jewish factory.
And they had some materials left.
So Schindler ordered that every [INAUDIBLE] should get a cut of suit for men and some cuts for clothing for women.
So we had a little package there.
So Margot says, I'll sew machine.
So she sat down, and she made herself a blouse and a skirt to be dressed a little bit not from these things.
And we were there for a few days.
And then I moved-- I walked into the kitchen to another room.
And I saw a picture with the SS men uniform.
And I says, who's this?
She says, that's my husband, but I don't know where he is, and if he is alive.
So I came back and I says, I don't want to stay here.
So we went to the train.
There was no train moving.
Only thing that-- it went some coals that were moving.
So we got in a coal train, all four of us-- Margot, my brother, and this other man.
He was from Krakow.
And we went there.
In the meantime, on the way, there was the-- the tracks, the rail tracks were cut off because from bombs.
So we have to walk over another 10, 11 miles to the other side.
And the other side was another train like this.
So we got on another train.
And we came back to Krakow.
From Krakow, we went to Tarnow.
This man went off in Krakow, because he was from Krakow.
And we went to Tarnow.
In Tarnow, then we came in with the train.
We had this little bundle.
And I was still in this-- my striped suit.
So I asked where's there's-- if there's a Jewish community.
So they told me in this street, there's a Jewish community.
So I went there.
And I-- and in the front of this building, I saw two people that I knew from before the war.
They were-- one was a customer of mine.
So they had an apartment.
They were already-- they were liberated in January from Auschwitz.
So they-- or no, from [INAUDIBLE].
So they were there already.
So they had an apartment.
So they took us into this apartment.
And I had-- we had still some money still left.
So I asked him what kind of money there's in Poland.
So he says zloty.
So I ask him what is-- what can you buy for a zloty So he told me.
So he lent me 500 zlotys.
And I went to the marketplace.
And I bought a quart of milk and a butter and a bread.
And we went home.
First of all, we ate.
Then I went to a marketplace.
And I bought a suit, a used suit for myself.
And that's the way we started out.
And then we still lived in town for three or four months.
And then we moved to Krakow.
In Krakow, we bought an apartment.
We lived in Krakow for one year.
After a year, September, we moved to France.
Black market.
Exactly-- you go on in place and you buy dollars.
You buy gold.
And nobody has anything in the packet.
Everything is on the wall.
You buy it.
And the market is open until 12-- not officially open.
The dealing goes in until 12 o'clock.
After 12 o'clock, everybody disappeared.
Because the police came in every hour, so often and they-- it circled everybody.
And they start searching.
So nobody had anything.
But you could sell and buy dollars as much as you want.
Everything was on-- on your word.
If you didn't keep your word, you didn't have any place there anymore.
So you could have buy dollars or sell dollars.
In the afternoon was double the price.
You have to leave it or you have to take it.
And in the afternoon, we went out some arranged this, whoever bought got the dollars, whoever paid.
So was the dealing for a year.
And all you had to work with was what you had saved throughout the-- your experiences in the camps, the money that you saved?
Or, was there anything else that you had to work with?
Yes, we had saved materials, too.
When we came into town, if you took out materials-- we had materials under the floor.
You see, you have-- a floor has parquets, right?
Under the parquets is there 2 by 2's big things.
And in the meantime, between the [INAUDIBLE] everything is-- is empty.
So you pushed in materials.
You opened up a square piece, and you pushed in materials and stick it in further down so you can-- as much as you want for them.
And then you put back in the parquets.
People-- Polish people were living in this apartment.
When I came in, I told them I have a right to my apartment.
I can chase you out.
But if you behave, I'm not going to take your apartment away.
So they say, OK.
But I have to have police with me, because I was-- I was afraid of what-- if they're going to kill me there.
So I took police along, and I pay them off.
They came and opened up.
When they saw what we took out, they start pulling their hairs out.
Because they were sitting on them, on this material.
And they didn't know anything.
It was-- everything was-- material was very, very expensive after the war.
There was a shortage of everything
During the first two years.
Oh, I forgot to say about they-- how they took away my business.
Yes.
In 1940-- in 1939, they said everybody has to put in the-- the inventory from the materials.
We had a big inventory, because that was before the season.
We had two rooms full of merchandise up all the way up to the ceiling.
We had two rooms.
You could go in from the-- from the outside.
That was in the courtyard.
Could go into each one from the outside and also could go in from one room to the other one through a small door.
So I had keys to these doors into these doors.
In the meantime, I had-- for some reason-- we never had double keys.
But for some reason, before the war, something came up that I make double keys.
I don't know why until today I made double keys.
And when put in the inventory, some retail stores had the permission to open up their stores and to sell, naturally only to German people or to people that work for Germans.
They had to have [NON-ENGLISH], which they call it-- like stamps or some, whatever it is.
You didn't get the permission, because that was a wholesale place.
One-- the day before [INAUDIBLE], a German like Schindler, a-- a-- what's called a [NON-ENGLISH], came up to my brother's apartment, my sister-in-law.
And he say he want keys.
So she sent a little boy to call me.
I came up.
And my sister-in-law start crying, taking away.
He says, what, you don't have to worry about.
The fuhrer will give you Madagascar.
You're going to go to Madagascar.
You get your money over there, and you're going to live like a king.
That was their-- their imagination that the fuhrer will do for us.
Fuhrer, that means Hitler.
So I came in.
And he says he wants the keys.
And he had a piece of paper.
He didn't show me to read, but he waved like this.
I have authority to take over your merchandise.
So I gave him the keys.
And he asked me, do we have double keys?
And I says, yes.
I give him the keys from the other door.
But I still had a second set of keys.
And he took the keys, and he left.
That was-- it must've been in February, or March, a day before Passover.
No, that was-- he came for the keys the day before Passover.
So the night before Passover, I says to my brother, to Chaim-- we didn't want to bother my older brother, because he had a family.
So he says, we're going to go in and-- first of all, what we registered was about 60% only.
There were two reasons.
We were afraid if they take it out and they see that we didn't register everything, we can be punished.
Second thing, if they take out everything and we don't have anything to live-- so we have to do something.
So we went in the building.
The building was closed at 10 o'clock.
The janitor locked the building.
So we went in a quarter before 10 o'clock.
And we hide in a corner.
And he locked the door.
Then we went into a basement.
He opened up a basement, which we knew-- we look for basement that's nobody's.
And we opened up a basement.
And during the night, everybody in the building was all quite already-- 2 o'clock in the morning.
Everybody after the-- the ceremony, the dinners, went to sleep.
There were businesses and there were apartments in this building.
So we opened up the door.
We start taking merchandise.
Balls were big balls.
The winter material was big.
The summer material was a little smaller, but every ball had about 40, 45 yards.
So we took out a lot of these balls and put it in this basement.
And I put a lock on this basement, and we walked out.
Figured if they find it, nobody knows who's this is.
If they don't find it-- on May the 8th and the 9th, it was-- I-- I remember the days, because it was the days when the German army occupied Belgium and Holland.
He had a man sitting there and taking out our merchandise.
And he came-- this [INAUDIBLE] came every hour so often see how this works.
And he says to this guy-- this guy was a Jewish man, but he was also from Germany.
He said, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means, did you hear the last-- the last news, that our-- our army occupied Belgium, Holland?
That's why I remember the date.
What year exactly?
It was 1940.
And we took-- after they took out all the merchandise, he gave me back the keys.
So we went in.
My brother and I went in again during the night.
And we took out the merchandise back to our place.
I figured it doesn't have to be the basements.
And little by little, we made a special box that we went in and I-- take a whole ball of material.
And we had somebody like a porter tell him-- gave them a good tip.
And he went and he took it and brought it to our apartment.
From this apartment, we start selling.
There was some women came in.
And they bought 6, 8 yards of goods or 10 yards of goods.
And they wrapped it around themselves and put in a coat on top of it.
And they walked.
We used to joke.
It says, the same woman gets pregnant every day.
Of course, she looked like pregnant.
It was very hot days, but she has to have the coat on.
That was black market.
So that's the way we-- things that we bought for this morning, we always bought some dollars-- dollars or gold.
After the war, we found this, whatever we had buried.
And we had-- some of them we had in the camp alone.
That's all we had.
We had the silver things that we had.
That wasn't [INAUDIBLE].
This was our-- from our house.
In Krakow we were until September 1946.
We sold the apartment.
And we moved to France.
But we bought visas to France.
And-- and you couldn't go into France unless you had a visa someplace.
So we had a visa to-- we had an affidavit to America.
So we both-- we went to the German-- to the French consul.
And we paid him off, and he gave us visas transit.
And when we came to France, we registered to go to America.
But America didn't let us in, because it was Polish-- Polish quota.
Polish quota was very behind.
Margot could go after a few months, because she was German quota.
She was born in Germany.
But she didn't want to go without me.
And I couldn't go.
So we-- they said that's going to take two years.
In the meantime, it took seven years.
And after seven years, finally we got the visa.
And we came to America.
In France, it was very nice.
We lived-- three daughters were born in France.
We had a business.
We had an apartment.
And we had a manufacturer from shirts.
And it was nice.
The only thing is, first of all, Margot wanted to be with her family.
We didn't have any family in France.
And secondly, at that time was a little dangerous that French will become Communist.
It was-- I-- somebody by the name Thorez, Maurice Thorez.
He was-- he wanted to run for Prime Minister, and he was a Communist.
So it was, at that time, was a little dangerous, this, too.
So we decided to leave when we could leave for America.
So we give up this.
Yes, in France, yes, yes.
We had a manufacturing from shirts.
We started-- we have to do something.
So we started out.
We bought materials and we opened up.
Our-- our name was-- I still have paper is I can show.
If you're interested, I can show you-- the papers, that is.
[NON-ENGLISH] it was called-- [NON-ENGLISH].
It was the manufacture from shirts.
And we had women working for us sewing these shirts.
And we had sales.
We had a partner.
And then by then, we separated.
We had ourselves.
And after we got the visas, so we liquidated everything.
And we sold our apartment, and we left.
My wife, my brother, and I.
My brother left three months earlier.
You see, we have-- there was another thing.
My brother-- you have to have an affidavit from America to-- somebody has to guarantee for you.
So he had an affidavit from an aunt, Margot's aunt, and also from her brother.
My brother didn't have anything.
And I had an uncle in America, which he left-- I never saw him.
He left about 15 years before I was born.
But I knew about him.
Because he left by himself, and then his wife was in our house.
And she left from our house.
And his children left from our house.
There was all-- he was my mother's brother.
But he was much older than my mother.
And I never knew him, because he left in 19-- I don't know, maybe '10-- but much before I was born, maybe.
So and I had another uncle which also came to America-- his mother.
And he died.
Two months after he came to America, he died for some reason.
So he came with a daughter.
So after the war, when I came back to Tarnow, I went to the Judenrat.
And they had a box.
All the mail which came from any place in the world for Jews was piled up in this box.
So I start looking through this box.
And I found a letter to my aunt from this-- his brother's wife.
So nobody was alive at that time.
So I took out the letter.
And I opened up and it was from this uncle.
And he ask about whoever lives in the family.
So he was at that time 90 years old.
And I saw in the handwriting there was like-- like shaking his hand.
So I opened up the letter.
And I read it, and then I entered in my letter.
And I showed-- put in that picture from Margot and me and from my brother.
When he got-- he lived in Pennsylvania.
When he had-- when he got this letter, he wrote back a letter.
And I told him in this letter that I'm going to America.
But my brother cannot go, because he doesn't have any affidavit.
And he was from the olden days.
The olden days was when you wanted to bring somebody, you have to go to the HIAS and pay in so and so much money.
So we went-- he brought back a letter and says, I should give him the information from my brother.
So I gave him all the information.
Huh?
HIAS is an organization like the-- the-- today, it's, for instance, what's called the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] immigration-- immigration office, the Jewish immigration office.
That was the HIAS.
What's-- I don't remember what it stands for.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, he went to this organization.
And he paid.
In the meantime, he passed away.
So my brother-- the affidavit is good for two years.
After two years, you have to renew it.
So when my brother's time came up to go to America, his affidavit was not valid.
So the HIAS went to my uncle's children and asked them to sign up.
So they said, they cannot sign for anybody-- for somebody that they don't know.
They didn't know us at all.
So the HIAS was obligated.
Because they took money from him, they were obligated.
So they made out an affidavit.
So my brother came to America three months before we came.
And when you came to America, what did you do?
I came to America.
I got a job as I cutter, a clothing cutter by Kuppenheimer, you know?
It was a company, a big company, by the name Kuppenheimer here.
It was a 1953.
Chicago.
I came-- we came to Chicago, because Margot had these relatives and brothers in Chicago.
So we came [INAUDIBLE].
And I look for a job.
So I got a job at Kuppenheimer as a cutter.
So I work for Kuppenheimer for 19 years.
And then Kuppenheimer it was-- came in the 1960s when nobody needed any suits anymore.
Kuppenheimer was a very expensive place, one of the highest manufacturing place in Chicago, better than Hart Schaffner Marx.
So when it came to the '60s when nobody-- when everybody was wearing blue jeans and so on, so they went out of business.
They closed up.
So after this-- and I was 19 years.
And I didn't have enough for pension.
And I wasn't older.
I was 62 at that time when they closed it.
So the union give me a job at Hart Schaffner Marx for another two years.
And after two years, this place went out of business.
This wasn't the Hart Schaffner Marx That was the Hart Schaffner Marx, a-- a-- a-- subsidy.
Because they worked only for Sears.
That was owned by Hart Schaffner Marx, but was called [INAUDIBLE].
It was called [INAUDIBLE].
They worked only for Sears Roebuck.
So after two years, they went out of business.
So the-- the union told me to quit.
So I was 64 years old.
So I got my pension at that time.
And when did you start to tell your story?
After the movie came out.
Before the movie came-- actually, when the book came out, our rabbi in our synagogue made a Sunday breakfast for the people.
And he says he's going to read some stories from from the book from the book that came out in 1982.
When I heard this, I says, We were on When he heard this, he got very excited.
And he invited Regine and Regine should come.
And she should choose some sections from this book to read.
And she read from all those people.
Then became known that we were But before, nobody cared.
If I would have told somebody, you know, nobody would pay attention.
Because that didn't mean anything.
After the movie came out, then they start-- when the movie came out, Regine said that she wants us-- we should be interviewed.
I said, not from you.
So she sent another man from the station.
And he called us.
And he made the interview.
After this came-- when this came on the air, right away-- the the all-- everybody, everybody start calling.
Regine is our daughter.
She's working for WBB--
